chain_of_thought: Top native frame below libc is byte_array_to_bson_string, which is just a thunk calling function pointer PTR_byte_array_to_bson_string_00109fa0; underlying implementation not present in this lib view, so exact memmove usage can’t be seen.

Next frames (bson_array_from_bytes_len, bson_object_from_bytes_len) are also thunks via function pointers; real parsing/decoding logic is similarly hidden, preventing detailed intra-function data-flow analysis of src/dst/len passed to memmove.

JNI entry Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes is fully decompiled. It allocates a 0x28-byte buffer, then obtains a pointer (uVar2) and a length (iVar1) for the Java byte[] via VM helper callbacks, and passes (__ptr,uVar2,(long)iVar1) directly to bson_object_from_bytes_len, which ultimately leads to memmove.

There is no bounds check or validation in the JNI wrapper on iVar1 with respect to the fixed 0x28-byte destination allocation; length comes directly from the Java-provided byte[] (hence attacker-controlled via fuzzer or app input).

If the underlying implementation in PTR_bson_object_from_bytes_len_00109e38 or PTR_byte_array_to_bson_string_00109fa0 assumes dest has at least len bytes and uses memmove accordingly (typical pattern when decoding into a stack/heap struct), then any Java byte[] longer than 0x28 will overflow the 0x28-byte buffer, causing an out-of-bounds write and memmove crash.

We cannot see the exact body of the function pointer implementations, but the combination of: crash in memmove, direct unbounded propagation of user length into native parser, and clearly undersized fixed allocation for a variable-length input strongly indicates a classic buffer overflow vulnerability.

Because the Java side can supply arbitrary-length byte arrays, this is realistically triggerable in production (e.g., malicious or corrupted BSON payloads), and can lead to heap corruption and potential code execution, beyond a simple benign abort.
reasons: JNI wrapper allocates fixed 0x28-byte buffer but passes unbounded user-controlled length into native BSON decoder
Crash occurs in memmove downstream of bson_object_from_bytes_len, consistent with buffer overflow when Java byte[] length exceeds 0x28
Underlying implementations are via function pointers so exact code is hidden, but call pattern and allocator mismatch strongly suggest out-of-bounds write, not harness-only misuse
recommendations: Inspect and decompile the implementations behind PTR_bson_object_from_bytes_len_00109e38 and PTR_byte_array_to_bson_string_00109fa0 to confirm how memmove length is computed and applied.
Change JNI wrapper to allocate destination buffer based on validated input length or enforce a strict maximum length before calling bson_object_from_bytes_len.
Add explicit bounds checks in native BSON decoding routines before any memmove/memcpy, and add tests/fuzzing with oversized BSON inputs to validate fixes.
assumptions: Length iVar1 returned from VM helper reflects Java byte[] length and is attacker-controllable via input.
bson_object_from_bytes_len/byte_array_to_bson_string do not internally clamp or revalidate length against the 0x28-byte buffer allocated by the JNI wrapper.
Crash memmove frame corresponds to copying into or from the 0x28-byte structure, not some unrelated buffer.
limitations: Implementations behind the function pointers (PTR_bson_object_from_bytes_len_00109e38, PTR_byte_array_to_bson_string_00109fa0) are not visible, so exact memmove arguments cannot be confirmed.
Stack trace does not show precise memmove parameters or address ranges, so overflow direction and exploitability details are inferred.
Analysis is limited to libbson-c-lib.so; any additional validation in other layers or Java-side API contracts is not visible.

chain_of_thought: Top native frame is memmove, next is byte_array_to_bson_string, but that function is an indirect thunk calling PTR_byte_array_to_bson_string_00109fa0; same for bson_object_from_bytes_len which is also a thunk. I don’t have the implementation of the function pointer targets in this binary, so I can’t see the actual memmove arguments. However, I can inspect the JNI entry Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes which calls bson_object_from_bytes_len.

In the JNI function, it allocates 0x28 bytes into __ptr, then obtains a pointer uVar2 and an int iVar1 from two function pointers off param_1 (JNIEnv-like vtable) using param_3 (the Java byte[]). It then calls bson_object_from_bytes_len(__ptr, uVar2, (long)iVar1). After the call, it frees uVar2 via another function pointer, and if lVar3 != iVar1 it logs an error, frees __ptr and returns -1 cast as pointer.

This indicates that bson_object_from_bytes_len is passed: dst=__ptr (fixed 0x28 buffer), src=uVar2 (points to bytes from Java array), len=(long)iVar1 (length of array). There is no bounds checking against the fixed 0x28 size here. If the implementation of bson_object_from_bytes_len uses memmove or similar to copy len bytes into the 0x28 buffer without validating that len <= 0x28, that would be a classic stack/heap buffer overflow. However, we do not see its body because it’s an indirect call through PTR_bson_object_from_bytes_len_00109e38.

We do know from the stack trace that memmove is being called from byte_array_to_bson_string, then from bson_object_from_bytes_len, which strongly suggests that the memmove arguments derive from what JNI provided. byte_array_to_bson_string is another indirect thunk, so we also cannot see its internals. Nevertheless, byte_array_to_bson_string takes no explicit args in the thunk, so the real function likely has its own parameters or uses globals; but bson_object_from_bytes_len definitely receives user-controlled length and pointer.

Taint/backward analysis: the length iVar1 comes from a JNIEnv vtable call (**(code **)(*param_1 + 0x558))(param_1,param_3); this very likely is GetArrayLength(env, jbyteArray), so iVar1 is exactly the Java array size, fully attacker-controlled via fuzz input. uVar2 comes from another JNIEnv call, likely GetByteArrayElements or GetPrimitiveArrayCritical, returning a pointer into the array storage. Those are safe JNI APIs but they confer user-controlled data. None of the values are clamped or checked before passing to bson_object_from_bytes_len. Thus, any lack of bounds checking inside bson_object_from_bytes_len or byte_array_to_bson_string could lead to memmove overflows.

Allocator crash reason is simply reported as memmove, not an ASan summary, so we don’t see explicit heap corruption metadata. But the fact that we’re fuzzing arbitrary bytes and we hit a memmove crash via this path is strong evidence that the implementation does something unsafe with byte-array length. There is no sign the crash is due to the harness misusing JNI; param_3 is a normal Java byte array, and the JNI code is plausible production code that returns a pointer to a BSON object struct.

Because we lack the decompiled body of bson_object_from_bytes_len/byte_array_to_bson_string, we can’t fully prove the exact bug (e.g., memcpy dst<len, use‑after‑free, etc.). However, the crash in memmove is a strong indicator of memory corruption or invalid pointer arising from operations on untrusted byte arrays. Given that the JNI wrapper passes attacker-controlled length and pointer directly into native parsing code without any pre-validation, this is highly likely to be a real, reachable memory-safety vulnerability in the BSON parser implementation, not a harness artifact.

Severity: If the memmove overflow is on heap or struct buffer used for BSON objects, this can yield heap corruption and potentially code execution, especially in a long-lived process like an Android app. So it should be rated high severity. Confidence slightly below 0.9 because we cannot see the exact implementation, but the combination of memmove crash, unbounded native copy, and JNI path from untrusted input is strong evidence.
reasons: Crash in memmove reached via BSON parsing functions suggests memory corruption in native parser
JNI wrapper passes attacker-controlled byte array pointer and length directly into bson_object_from_bytes_len with no bounds checking
Implementation of bson_object_from_bytes_len and byte_array_to_bson_string is opaque (function pointers), but stack trace and crash location strongly imply unsafe memmove based on untrusted length
recommendations: Reverse engineer implementations behind PTR_bson_object_from_bytes_len_00109e38 and PTR_byte_array_to_bson_string_00109fa0 to confirm memmove argument relationships and add strict bounds checks on copy lengths
Ensure bson_object_from_bytes_len validates that the requested copy length does not exceed destination buffer sizes before calling memmove/memcpy
Add defensive checks in the JNI layer to limit maximum BSON input size and reject malformed or oversized byte arrays before passing them to native parsing code
assumptions: JNI function table calls at offsets 0x5c0 and 0x558 correspond to typical byte-array accessors returning pointer and length
The crashing memmove uses arguments that derive from the Java byte array length and contents
limitations: Implementations of bson_object_from_bytes_len and byte_array_to_bson_string are not visible due to indirect function pointers
Exact nature of the memmove failure (overflow vs invalid pointer) cannot be conclusively determined from current data

chain_of_thought: Top native frame is memmove, next is byte_array_to_bson_string in libbson-c-lib.so; need to see how memmove is used and where its length comes from.

Decompiling byte_array_to_bson_string/bson_*_from_bytes_len shows only thunks via function pointers PTR_*; real implementations are elsewhere (probably in same lib or linked in), but not directly visible, so we cannot inspect the memmove call site itself.

However, we can see the JNI entry Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes, which allocates a 0x28 byte buffer, obtains a pointer and a length iVar1 for the Java byte[] via two virtual-like function calls on param_1, then calls bson_object_from_bytes_len(__ptr,uVar2,(long)iVar1).

Thus the length argument to the internal parser/bson_object_from_bytes_len is exactly the byte[] length as reported by the JNI environment; there is no extra bounds restriction besides the heap allocation for __ptr, which is a fixed 0x28 and unrelated to iVar1.

Inside the hidden bson_object_from_bytes_len/byte_array_to_bson_string implementation, a memmove is called and crashes. Because the API passes (ptr,len) where len is fully controlled by attacker-provided Java byte[] length and the destination buffer appears independent (e.g., small stack/local buffer or fixed-size struct), an out-of-bounds memmove on attacker-controlled length is highly plausible.

The JNI wrapper does a post-check: if (lVar3 != iVar1) { log and free(__ptr); return -1; }. lVar3 is a length returned by the parser; this does not guard against out-of-bounds inside the parser, it only verifies that all input bytes were consumed. No sanity checks on iVar1 size or content are performed before calling into bson_object_from_bytes_len.

The fuzz harness calls the real JNI entry directly with arbitrary bytes, which is a realistic usage model (an app can call this JNI with any byte[]), so this is not a harness-only artifact. A malformed or very large/evil BSON blob can cause memmove to misbehave if internal length fields are trusted without validation.

We cannot see the exact memmove arguments because the core bson implementation is via unresolved function pointers, but given the crash in memmove and typical BSON parsing patterns (copying variable-length strings/arrays based on lengths inside the buffer) with no observed outer bounds enforcement, this is strongly indicative of an out-of-bounds read/write driven by untrusted data.
Therefore, classify as a likely memory-corruption vulnerability (CWE-787/788) with high severity, though not absolutely confirmed due to missing decompilation of the pointer target functions.
reasons: Crash occurs inside memmove called from BSON parsing helper byte_array_to_bson_string, indicating buffer movement based on data length.
JNI wrapper passes attacker-controlled byte[] data and length directly into bson_object_from_bytes_len without size checks, enabling untrusted lengths to reach memmove.
Internal bson_*_from_bytes_len implementations are hidden behind function pointers, but the pattern and crash location are consistent with out-of-bounds memory access in a parser on untrusted input.
recommendations: Instrument and decompile the target of PTR_bson_object_from_bytes_len_00109e38/PTR_byte_array_to_bson_string_00109fa0 to confirm and locate the exact memmove call and add strict bounds checks on source and destination.
Add explicit validation in Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes to limit acceptable byte[] sizes and to defensively verify internal length fields during BSON parsing.
Add ASan/UBSan builds and targeted fuzzing of BSON decoding functions directly (without JNI) to reproduce and minimize this memmove crash and guide a precise fix.
assumptions: The Java byte[] passed into the JNI method is fully attacker-controllable in realistic application contexts.

The internal bson_object_from_bytes_len/byte_array_to_bson_string implementations use memmove with lengths derived from the input buffer and/or the provided length parameter without complete bounds checking.
The fixed 0x28-byte allocation for __ptr is not the immediate destination of the failing memmove but other internal buffers are being overrun.
limitations: Actual implementation of bson_object_from_bytes_len and byte_array_to_bson_string is hidden behind function pointers, so the precise memmove argument relationships are not visible.
Stack trace lacks register/argument dump at the memmove site, preventing definitive identification of source/destination/length.
No proof-of-concept input is provided to correlate specific malformed BSON patterns with the crash behavior.

chain_of_thought: Top native frame is memmove; first app-level frame below is byte_array_to_bson_string in libbson-c-lib.so, called from bson_object_from_bytes_len (and from bson_array_from_bytes_len). We have full decompilation of bson_object_from_bytes_len; byte_array_to_bson_string is an imported/indirect wrapper through PTR_byte_array_to_bson_string_00109fa0, so its body is not visible, but its arguments and call sites are.

Within bson_object_from_bytes_len, in the case for BSON type 2 (string), the code reads a 32-bit little-endian length iVar6 from the input buffer (local_88/local_90). It checks that this uVar11 (iVar6) is <= remaining bytes (local_90) before proceeding. If so, it calls pppppcVar8 = (char*****)byte_array_to_bson_string(local_88, uVar11 - 1). That is, it passes the POINTER to the current location in the BSON byte stream, and a LENGTH equal to the claimed string length minus 1 (to exclude the trailing null), to byte_array_to_bson_string. This is the only place we see byte_array_to_bson_string used in the object parser; xrefs also show it used from bson_array_from_bytes_len in a similar pattern.

The process termination is reported as 'memmove', which typically means memmove hit an invalid buffer (e.g., via memcpy/memmove inside libc). Since byte_array_to_bson_string is just an indirect wrapper, it likely uses memmove/memcpy internally to copy the BSON string bytes into a new heap buffer or similar. Therefore the key question is whether the src/dst/len arguments to that internal memmove can become invalid or out-of-bounds due to attacker-controlled data. We do not see its implementation, so we must reason from its call interface and surrounding checks.

At the call site, src == local_88, which always points inside the original input buffer (param_2) and is advanced only under the control of local_90 and explicit parsing logic. The parser maintains invariants such that it only reads within remaining bytes; before this string case, we have checked that uVar11 <= local_90. Thus local_88..local_88+uVar11-1 lies fully within the remaining buffer. That strongly suggests that the memmove in byte_array_to_bson_string is copying from a valid region inside the input; the only remaining risk is destination and length.

The length argument passed to byte_array_to_bson_string is uVar11 - 1 (unsigned). uVar11 is a ulong converted from a 32-bit signed iVar6. There is a guard 'if (3 < local_90) { iVar6 = read_int32_le(&local_88); uVar11 = (ulong)iVar6; local_90 -= 4; if (uVar11 <= local_90) { ... } }'. This ensures uVar11 <= local_90, but it does NOT check anything about the reasonableness of iVar6: very large positive lengths are filtered out by the uVar11 <= local_90 constraint, so the effective length is always bounded by remaining buffer size. Negative values, interpreted as signed, become large unsigned values if cast, but the code uses uVar11 <= local_90 where local_90 is non-negative and much smaller (remaining input size). For a negative iVar6, uVar11 would be a very large 64-bit number, thus the check fails (uVar11 <= local_90 is false), and the string parsing block is skipped and fall-through leads to error handling. So the length passed into byte_array_to_bson_string is always <= local_90, and local_90 itself is the remaining valid bytes in the buffer. In other words: src range is well-bounded at the call site, and length is bounded by remaining bytes. This eliminates straightforward out-of-bounds read into memmove at this level.

What about destination? byte_array_to_bson_string presumably allocates a new buffer based on the provided length and returns a pointer to it (pppppcVar8). The calling code then does 'sVar9 = strlen((char *)pppppcVar8); local_d8 = sVar9 + 5; local_e0 = 2; local_e8 = pppppcVar8; bson_object_put_element(&local_b8,pvVar3,&local_e8,sVar9 + 1); free(pppppcVar8);'. If byte_array_to_bson_string implements the typical behavior of copying 'len' bytes and appending a null terminator into an allocated buffer of size len+1, then as long as len equals uVar11-1, and uVar11-1 <= local_90, there is no overflow. If byte_array_to_bson_string instead miscomputes its allocation or uses memmove with a length greater than allocated size, that would be an internal bug in that routine, not obviously caused by unvalidated attacker-controlled length (since we pass a bounded len). However, we cannot see its body, so we cannot prove or disprove such an internal bug without more disassembly. Still, from the interface and guards, the app code calling it behaves correctly regarding bounds.

The crash is triggered by a fuzz harness feeding arbitrary byte sequences into Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes. That JNI bridge allocates a 0x28-byte struct, uses Java byte[] 'param_3' length (via a VM call) to determine param_3 to bson_object_from_bytes_len, and then checks that the returned parsed length lVar3 equals iVar1 (the byte array length). If not, it logs and returns -1. So lengths passed into bson_object_from_bytes_len are exact input size and fully influenced by attacker, but the parser itself rigorously checks that any internal length fields in the BSON enjoy uVar11 <= remaining bytes before decoding strings, nested objects, arrays, etc. We also see similar length-based recursion into bson_object_from_bytes_len and bson_array_from_bytes_len with return values checked; unexpected sizes cause error paths rather than unchecked copying.

Given that the termination is in memmove (allocator / libc), but the visible app code feeding lengths and pointers into the lower-level byte_array_to_bson_string and recursive parsers performs appropriate bounds checks on attacker-controlled fields, the evidence for a genuine memory safety vulnerability is weak. The likely causes include: (1) an internal implementation bug inside the imported byte_array_to_bson_string function (e.g., mis-handling of zero-length or specific edge cases), which would still be a library vulnerability but we lack visibility; (2) misuse or unrealistic input conditions from the fuzzer, possibly provoking undefined behavior in the library (e.g., null pointers), though here src is never null and length is validated; or (3) a sanitizer/harness issue attributing the crash generically to memmove even though the higher-level parameters are sane.

Because we do not see any actual unchecked memcpy/memmove with attacker-controlled len/source/dest in the decompiled app code; because every call to byte_array_to_bson_string from bson_object_from_bytes_len is guarded by stringent bounds checks on the string length relative to the remaining bytes; and because we lack the implementation of byte_array_to_bson_string itself to confirm a bug, the most conservative classification is that there is no *demonstrated* vulnerable pattern in the app-level code, though a deeper binary analysis of PTR_byte_array_to_bson_string_00109fa0 would be required to fully exonerate the library. Under the given instructions, without clear taintable path to an unsafe operation, this should be classified as not-a-confirmed vulnerability, lean toward harness/internal issue.

Impact-wise, if there *were* a bug in byte_array_to_bson_string, it could permit out-of-bounds read/write via memmove and be high severity (CWE-787), but since we can't see it and the interface is used safely, assigning such a CWE would be speculative. Hence we avoid calling it a proven vulnerability. The affected library is libbson-c-lib.so, and the call path is fuzz_one_input -> Java_com_livio_BSON_BsonEncoder_bson_1object_1from_1bytes -> bson_object_from_bytes_len -> byte_array_to_bson_string -> memmove.
reasons: memmove crash occurs inside indirect byte_array_to_bson_string implementation, which is not visible; callers enforce strict length <= remaining-bytes checks before passing attacker-controlled data
bson_object_from_bytes_len validates string length fields against buffer size, preventing straightforward out-of-bounds reads/writes in visible code
JNI bridge passes full Java byte[] length and checks parsed length for consistency, indicating sane use of the parser; remaining suspicion lies only in opaque library internals
recommendations: Disassemble and audit the implementation behind PTR_byte_array_to_bson_string_00109fa0 and its use of memmove for edge cases (len=0, very small buffers)
Run additional targeted fuzzing with ASan/UBSan on libbson-c-lib.so focusing on BSON type 2 strings and deep nesting to attempt to reproduce and localize crashes
If source is available, review byte_array_to_bson_string for off-by-one or allocation-size vs. copy-length mismatches and add explicit assertions or bounds checks
assumptions: byte_array_to_bson_string allocates a buffer of at least the requested len (uVar11-1) plus terminator and copies only that many bytes
local_90 accurately tracks remaining bytes and is initialized to the original buffer length passed from JNI
Crash report’s top frame memmove indeed corresponds to the call chain observed in decompiled code
limitations: Implementation of byte_array_to_bson_string is not decompiled, so internal bugs there cannot be ruled out
Exact memmove parameters at the crash site are unknown; analysis is based on static reasoning from surrounding code
Assessment does not include bson_array_from_bytes_len body, which could have additional paths to byte_array_to_bson_string with different invariants
